system_prompt:
You are distilling one chapter of a guitar method book into a short summary.

The user will give you a chapter file with topic-organized verbatim quotes
plus the human-rationale notes for each. Write a one-paragraph summary (4-7
sentences) that captures:

  - what this chapter teaches (the techniques, concepts, rules, examples)
  - the master's characteristic moves visible in this chapter (if any)
  - the load-bearing terminology this chapter introduces

Reference the topic labels from the chapter file when useful. Do not invent
information not present in the chapter. If the chapter is biographical or
introductory rather than instructional, say so explicitly.

Output just the paragraph — no preamble, no JSON, no markdown formatting.
user_prompt:
Chapter 5: Harmonic Fields & Scale Modes

<<<CHAPTER FILE
# Chapter 5 — Harmonic Fields & Scale Modes

_Pages 196-205._

## Harmonic Field definition

> As we know, the majority of the chords are built from the superimposition of 3rd 's, and in this
>   chapter we will apply that concept over the Natural Major Scale. In other words, we will build
>   chords from each note from that scale forming what we call: diatonic chords. As we have 7
>   notes, we will have 7 chords as well.

**Significance (p. 177).** Defines how the harmonic field is constructed via tertian stacking on each scale degree, yielding seven diatonic chords.

## Roman numeral degrees

> Each chord, using the same order that they appear (triad or tetrad), is represented by a
>   roman numeral (upper-case numerals represent major chords, and lower-case numerals
>   represent minor chords): I, IIm, IIIm, IV, V, VIm, VIIm. Now that we organized the chords
>   into roman numerals, we will call them “Degrees".

**Significance (p. 177).** Establishes the upper/lowercase Roman numeral convention used throughout the book for diatonic analysis.

## The 7 Modes

> If we start the natural major scale each time from different note, we will have 7 different scales.
> We call them “The 7 Modes”. Each mode will receive a specific name and has its own
> harmonic characteristics.

**Significance (p. 178).** Defines modes as rotations of the natural major scale, anchoring the book's modal framework.

## Mode names per degree

> I = Ionian: C, D, E, F, G, A, B
>
> II = Dorian : D, E, F, G, A, B, C
>
> III = Phrygian: E, F, G, A, B, C, D
>
> IV = Lydian: F, G, A, B, C, D, E
>
> V = Mixolydian: G, A, B, C, D, E, F
>
> VI = Aeolian: A, B, C, D, E, F, G
>
> VII = Locrian: B, C, D, E, F, G, A

**Significance (p. 178).** Load-bearing reference mapping each degree to its mode and exact note set in C.

## Modes drive harmony and melody

> It is important to understand that from now on we will use the modes for everything, including
> harmony and melody, because they can show us all the possible tensions for each chord and
> also which is the correct scale for each situation. So, you must memorize the names and the
> scales notes from each one of the modes.

**Significance (p. 178).** States the book's central rule that modes determine both chord tensions and scale choice.

## Types of Notes in the Modes

> a) Chord Notes:
>                        46
>    - Triads (1, 3, 5) or Tetrads (1, 3, 5, 7).
>
> b) Extended Notes:
>   - Tension notes or coloring notes (9, 11, 13).
>
> c) Avoid Notes:
>   - Scale notes that cause harmonic conflict with the chord notes. Melodically they can sound
>     interesting in the middle of a melody, but never as an ending note.
>
> d) Characteristic Notes:
>   - Scale notes that give the mode it's characteristics.

**Significance (p. 178).** Four-category taxonomy of scale notes used to analyze every mode in the book.

## Avoid notes rule

> Scale notes that cause harmonic conflict with the chord notes. Melodically they can sound
>     interesting in the middle of a melody, but never as an ending note.

**Significance (p. 178).** Practical rule for avoid notes: usable mid-phrase, never as a resting tone.

## Major vs Minor Harmonic Fields

> 1) Natural Major Harmonic Field (also can be called Major Harmonic Field)
>
>
>   2) Natural Minor Harmonic Field (also can be called Minor Harmonic Field)

**Significance (p. 179).** Names the two harmonic-field organizing systems analyzed in the chapter.

## Relative major/minor sharing chords

> Summarizing, we will use the Natural Major Harmonic Field to organize the
>   “happy sounding songs” and the Natural Minor Harmonic Field to organize the “sad sounding
>   songs”. In the following pages, we will better understand that both harmonic fields use the same
>   chords, the only difference is the way you analyze them.

**Significance (p. 179).** Key conceptual rule: major and minor fields share chords; only the analytic frame differs.

## Accidentals on degrees

> Note: When necessary we can use sharps (#) or flats (b) together with the degrees.
>   Example: bII, bIII, #IV.....

**Significance (p. 179).** Establishes notation convention for chromatic Roman numeral degrees used later.

## Ionian characteristics

> Chord notes (triads/tetrads): Root, major 3rd, perfect 5th, major 7th
>       Extended notes: major 9th, perfect 11th, major 13th
>       Chord: Cmaj 7 , C 69
>                    9
>       Avoid notes: perfect 4th /octave when the chord is Cmaj7
>       Characteristic notes: perfect 4th /major 7th

**Significance (p. 181).** Reference card for Ionian's chord tones, tensions, avoid notes, and characteristic notes.

## Lydian characteristics

> Chord notes (triads/tetrads): Root, major 3rd, perfect 5th, major 7th
>       Extended notes: major 9th, augmented 11th, major 13th
>       Chord: Fmaj7Ã   , Fmaj 79
>       Avoid notes: octave on Fmaj7 chords
>       Characteristic notes: augmented 4th /major 7th

**Significance (p. 181).** Defines Lydian's #11 tension and characteristic augmented 4th that distinguishes it from Ionian.

## Mixolydian characteristics

> Chord notes (triads/tetrads): Root, major 3rd, perfect 5th, minor 7th
> Extended notes: major 9th, perfect 11th, major 13th
> Chord: G 713 ( 9 ) ,   GW9
> Avoid notes: perfect 4th
> Characteristic notes: perfect 4th/minor 7th

**Significance (p. 182).** Defines dominant-seventh mode profile and its avoid-note constraint on the perfect 4th.

## Locrian characteristics

> Chord notes (triads/tetrads): Root, minor 3rd, dim 5th, minor 7th
> Extended notes: minor 9th, perfect 11th, minor 13th
> Chord: Bª
> Avoid notes : minor 2nd
> Characteristic notes: minor 2nd /dim 5th /minor 7th

**Significance (p. 182).** Defines the half-diminished mode profile used for the VII chord and minor II analyses.

## Extended notes usage rule

> Not all extended notes of a chord must be in it.
>
> Many of them should only be played as the passing tones of a melody on the chord.

**Significance (p. 182).** Key rule clarifying that listed tensions are melodic options, not mandatory voicing components.

## Natural Minor Harmonic Field degrees

> DEGREE :          Im               IIm           bIII            IVm           Vm        bVI     bVII
>
>       TETRAD :          Ami7              Bª            Cmaj7          Dmi7          Emi7      Fmaj7    G7

**Significance (p. 183).** Authoritative degree-to-tetrad mapping for the natural minor harmonic field.

CHAPTER FILE>>>
